items.q: What is the difference between reliability and validity?
items.correct: Reliability is consistency; validity is whether it measures what it claims to.
items.misconception: They are essentially the same thing.
items.random_wrong: Reliability is for quantitative, validity for qualitative.
items.why: Students treat these as synonyms. A bathroom scale that adds 5kg every time is reliable (consistent) but not valid (wrong reading). You can be reliable without being valid.

items.correct: Every member of the target population has an equal chance of selection.
items.misconception: Just grabbing whoever is around (any haphazard method).
items.random_wrong: Picking participants who match the population profile.
items.why: Students treat 'random' as 'casual' or 'haphazard'. True randomness requires a defined sampling frame and a chance procedure (e.g., random number generator).

items.q: What is the capacity of short-term memory according to Miller?
items.why: Students confuse capacity (items) with duration (about 18-30 seconds without rehearsal). Miller's magic number describes how many CHUNKS, not how long.

items.q: Maguire (2000) found enlarged ___ in London taxi drivers:
items.why: It's Hippocampi. “Amygdala” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.
The answer examiners want is: 5-HTT and depression. “DRD4 and risk-taking” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: In what year did Maguire publish her London taxi drivers study?
items.why: Exact answer: 2000 AD. Estimation gets you near it; only the anchored number survives the exam hall.
